proposals.title: High-Fidelity Quantum Memory in a Bose-Einstein Condensate via Robust Hamiltonian Engineering
proposals.motivation: The development of efficient, long-lived quantum memories is a central challenge for quantum networking and distributed quantum computation. While atomic ensembles are promising candidates, thermal motion and inhomogeneous broadening in warm vapors limit storage fidelity and lifetime. A non-thermal, ultracold ensemble like a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) offers a pristine, coherent medium with minimal Doppler effects, potentially overcoming these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying Hamiltonian engineering techniques, such as dynamical decoupling sequences, to a BEC-based memory, we can suppress residual dephasing and control atom-atom interactions to achieve significantly higher storage fidelity and longer coherence times compared to thermal ensemble memories.
proposals.method: We will implement a quantum memory protocol using a BEC of rubidium-87 atoms trapped in a dynamically programmable optical tweezer array. The storage of photonic qubits will employ a lambda-type scheme using electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT). We will adapt robust Hamiltonian engineering methods, previously developed for interacting qudit systems, to design optimized control sequences (e.g., WAHUHA or MREV-8 variants) that actively compensate for inhomogeneous light shifts and mitigate deleterious interactions within the BEC during storage. The performance will be benchmarked using randomized benchmarking of memory operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new, high-performance platform for optical quantum memory, directly linking advances in ultracold quantum matter with quantum networking. It will provide quantitative benchmarks for coherence times and fidelities in a BEC memory and establish control protocols for managing interactions in ensemble-based quantum devices.
proposals.novelty: The integration of sophisticated Hamiltonian engineering control, a hallmark of Lukin's group's work on qudit systems, with the unique coherence properties of a BEC for quantum storage is a novel synthesis. Most prior BEC memory experiments have used simpler, static control, not active dynamical decoupling tailored for an interacting quantum degenerate gas.

proposals.title: High-Fidelity, On-Demand Quantum Memory via Dynamically Programmable Collective Excitations in a Rydberg Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Efficient quantum memories, which map photonic qubits into long-lived atomic coherences and retrieve them on demand, are a critical bottleneck for scalable quantum networks and repeaters. Pre-2026 demonstrations using diamond NV centers or atomic ensembles show promise but often trade efficiency for fidelity or bandwidth. A platform combining high optical depth, long coherence times, and precise, programmable control is needed to overcome these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a dynamically field-programmable array of neutral atoms coupled to a Rydberg state, we can engineer collective atomic excitations that enable near-deterministic, high-fidelity storage and retrieval of single photons through enhanced light-matter interaction and active error suppression.
proposals.method: We will implement the memory using a 2D array of Rb-87 atoms trapped in optical tweezers, with a Rydberg blockade mechanism to create collective atomic qubits. The storage protocol will combine robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques (inspired by Lukin's qudit work) to design optimized control pulses that map the photonic state into a protected dark state polariton. Retrieval will be triggered via a dynamically programmable circuit that applies local microwave and optical gates to the array, reversing the mapping process while suppressing noise and dephasing.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a quantum memory with simultaneous high efficiency (>90%) and high fidelity (>99%) for single photons, a key milestone for practical quantum networking. It will establish a novel architecture where memory control is integrated into the same programmable platform used for quantum processing, moving towards unified quantum network nodes.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely merges the high optical depth and programmability of neutral atom arrays with Rydberg-mediated interactions and advanced dynamical decoupling sequences, going beyond static ensemble memories or single-atom nodes to create a configurable, multi-qubit memory register.

proposals.title: Generalizing Quantum Memory Protocols: A Multi-Species Investigation of Alkaline-Earth(-like) Vapors for Enhanced Telecom Networking
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, quantum networking demonstrations using diamond quantum memories have shown promise but are limited by material-specific challenges. A robust, scalable quantum internet requires versatile, room-temperature memory platforms compatible with telecom wavelengths. Exploring alternative atomic vapors, particularly those with metastable states and telecom-compatible transitions, could unlock superior coherence times, higher bandwidth, and more flexible system integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the quantum storage protocols developed for diamond NV centers can be successfully adapted to other atomic species with alkaline-earth(-like) electronic structure (e.g., Sr, Yb, or Rb in a buffer gas), and that specific transitions in these species will yield superior performance metrics for telecom-band quantum networking.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged experimental approach using dynamically programmable neutral atom arrays. First, we will implement robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, akin to those used for interacting qudit systems, to design and optimize control sequences for new target species (e.g., Sr, Yb) loaded into optical tweezer arrays. Second, we will compile these sequences into high-fidelity gates and circuits to map, store, and retrieve photonic qubits via identified telecom-compatible transitions (e.g., clock transitions or transitions to metastable states), benchmarking performance against established diamond memory protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalized framework for evaluating and implementing atomic-vapor-based quantum memories. It will produce a comparative performance analysis of multiple species for key metrics like storage fidelity, bandwidth, and lifetime, directly informing hardware choices for future quantum networks. The results could identify a superior physical platform for practical, room-temperature quantum repeaters.
proposals.novelty: While individual species have been studied, a systematic, protocol-driven comparison of multiple atomic vapors for telecom quantum memory using the advanced control techniques of neutral atom arrays is novel. It moves beyond optimizing a single system to developing a transferable engineering methodology for quantum memory hardware.

proposals.title: Robust Quantum Sensing in Diamond via Dynamically Decoupled Control Sequences
proposals.motivation: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are leading platforms for quantum sensing, but their sensitivity is limited by environmental noise and control imperfections. Advanced pulse sequences from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), such as WAHUHA, MREV-8, and robust composite pulses, have been used for decades to suppress decoherence and control errors in spin systems, but their full potential for enhancing solid-state quantum sensors remains underexplored. Applying these techniques to NV centers could significantly improve their performance in demanding applications like nanoscale magnetic resonance imaging and network node operation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically adapting and optimizing advanced NMR pulse sequences for the NV center's specific Hamiltonian and noise environment, we can engineer a robust quantum control framework that extends coherence times, suppresses systematic errors, and enhances the signal-to-noise ratio for sensing protocols beyond the limits of standard dynamical decoupling.
proposals.method: We will employ a Hamiltonian engineering approach, treating the NV electronic spin and its coupled nuclear spins as a multi-qudit system. Using the group's expertise in compiling quantum circuits for neutral atoms, we will design and compile complex, multi-axis pulse sequences (e.g., concatenated or periodic dynamical decoupling) into high-fidelity microwave control waveforms for the NV center. These sequences will be tested on a single-NV platform to benchmark coherence enhancement and then integrated into sensing protocols (e.g., AC magnetometry) to directly measure gains in sensitivity and robustness against pulse imperfections and low-frequency noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new toolbox of robust control sequences specifically tailored for NV-center quantum sensing, directly translating decades of NMR expertise into the solid-state. It will provide quantitative benchmarks for sensitivity gains under realistic noise conditions and demonstrate a pathway to more reliable quantum sensors for integration into quantum networks, as explored in the group's telecom memory work.
proposals.novelty: While dynamical decoupling is standard, the systematic application and optimization of sophisticated, multi-axis NMR-style sequences like MREV-8 for NV center sensing is non-trivial and represents a significant advance over commonly used Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill (CPMG) sequences. The novelty lies in treating the NV as a complex, interacting qudit system for control design, moving beyond simple two-level approximations.

proposals.title: Quantum-Limited Readout for Neutral-Atom Processors via Superconducting Circuit Signal Processing
proposals.motivation: High-fidelity, single-shot readout is a critical bottleneck for scaling neutral-atom quantum processors. While superconducting qubit platforms have developed sophisticated quantum-limited amplification and filtering techniques to overcome similar signal-to-noise challenges, these methods have not been systematically adapted to the optical-frequency signals and distinct environmental noise spectra of neutral-atom arrays. Improving readout fidelity directly enhances the feasibility of complex quantum circuits and error correction in these dynamically programmable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and integrating quantum-limited parametric amplifiers and optimized filtering techniques from superconducting circuit architectures into the optical readout chain of a neutral-atom array processor, we can significantly suppress the added noise and increase the signal-to-noise ratio, leading to a measurable improvement in single-shot qubit readout fidelity.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a custom optical readout chain for a 87Rb atom array, incorporating a Josephson Parametric Amplifier (JPA) or Traveling-Wave Parametric Amplifier (TWPA) adapted for the relevant optical wavelengths via efficient frequency conversion. We will implement real-time, FPGA-based filtering algorithms (e.g., matched filtering and optimal quadrature discrimination) specifically tuned to the temporal profile of atomic fluorescence signals. The system's performance will be benchmarked against standard heterodyne detection on a dynamically field-programmable array, measuring fidelity gains across different array sizes and qubit states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new technical paradigm for high-fidelity optical readout in neutral-atom systems, directly translating a key enabling technology from superconducting qubits. It will provide a quantitative framework for noise suppression in atom-based quantum memories and processors, a crucial step towards their integration into quantum networks, as explored in Lukin's prior work on diamond memories and telecom networking.
proposals.novelty: The direct cross-platform translation of quantum-limited microwave amplification and real-time signal processing techniques to the optical domain for neutral-atom readout is a novel synthesis. While both fields advance independently, a dedicated effort to port these specific signal-processing methods to atomic systems, considering their unique Hamiltonian engineering and noise profiles, has not been reported.

proposals.title: Active Cryogenic Control for Enhanced Nuclear Spin Initialization and Gate Fidelity in Diamond NV-Nuclear Registers
proposals.motivation: NV-nuclear spin registers are promising quantum nodes, but their performance is limited by nuclear spin polarization rates and two-qubit gate fidelities. While millikelvin operation is known to extend electron spin coherence, its potential for enabling active, high-fidelity control akin to superconducting qubits remains unexplored. This gap exists because NV experiments at these temperatures typically rely on room-temperature control electronics, introducing noise and latency that negate the benefits of the cold bath.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating specialized, cryogenically-compatible microwave/RF control hardware directly within the millikelvin stage will enable active control paradigms for NV centers, leading to a significant improvement in nuclear spin polarization efficiency and two-qubit gate fidelity, beyond the passive gains from extended coherence.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a custom microwave delivery and detection system for a dilution refrigerator, integrating cryogenic amplifiers, filters, and impedance-matched stripline generators near the sample. Using a dynamically field-programmable array of NV centers, we will benchmark the performance of standard dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) sequences and engineered Hamiltonian gates (e.g., for robust CZ gates) against room-temperature control baselines. Key metrics will include polarization contrast, gate fidelity measured via randomized benchmarking, and the signal-to-noise ratio of electron spin readout.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new technical paradigm for high-performance NV-center quantum processors at millikelvin temperatures, moving beyond passive coherence preservation. It will provide quantitative performance trade-off data critical for scaling NV-nuclear registers and inform the design of integrated quantum memory and networking nodes, as explored in Lukin's prior work on diamond memories.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly adapting the active cryogenic control architecture from superconducting qubits to solid-state spin qubits, challenging the prevailing assumption that ultra-low temperatures are primarily for passive coherence. This approach investigates the system-level control advantages, not just the material-level benefits.

proposals.title: Demonstration of a Permanent Electron-Spin Quantum Bus for Robust Multi-Qudit Entanglement in Diamond
proposals.motivation: In solid-state spin systems like NV centers in diamond, the electron spin is typically used as a fragile, high-speed communication bus that is decoupled during nuclear spin memory operations to protect it from decoherence. This intermittent coupling limits the speed and parallelism of quantum operations. A continuously active, robust quantum bus could enable faster, more complex multi-qudit algorithms and more efficient quantum networking protocols, addressing a key bottleneck in scaling quantum processors.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying robust dynamical decoupling sequences directly to the electron spin while it remains permanently coupled to an ensemble of nuclear spin qudits, we can engineer a protected, always-on interaction channel. This will allow for the deterministic generation and stabilization of multi-qudit entangled states during concurrent computational gates.
proposals.method: We will use a single NV center in diamond with a surrounding 13C nuclear spin register. Instead of turning off the hyperfine coupling, we will keep the electron spin continuously resonant or near-resonant. We will design and implement Walsh-modulated dynamical decoupling sequences on the electron spin, inspired by recent Hamiltonian engineering work for interacting qudit systems, to suppress its decoherence while preserving specific, tunable components of the hyperfine interaction. This enables the compilation of quantum circuits where the electron acts as a permanent bus, mediating gates between nuclear spins via always-on couplings.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and validate a new paradigm for quantum processor architecture with a permanently active communication bus. It will provide a concrete protocol for generating and sustaining complex multi-qudit entanglement, directly impacting the development of quantum memories and processors with enhanced connectivity and speed, relevant for quantum networking applications.
proposals.novelty: Contrary to the standard approach of decoupling the electron bus to protect it, we propose to actively protect it *while* it is coupled, transforming it from a temporary resource into a permanent, robust component of the quantum processor. This merges concepts of robust control, qudit operations, and circuit compilation in a novel way.

proposals.title: High-Fidelity Single-Shot Readout of Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers via Closed-Loop Resonant Spin Locking
proposals.motivation: The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond is a leading platform for quantum sensing, networking, and memory, but its utility for scalable quantum information processing is limited by the fidelity and speed of non-optical spin readout. Conventional electrical or microwave readout schemes suffer from poor signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), preventing high-fidelity single-shot measurement, which is a prerequisite for fault-tolerant quantum error correction as defined in pre-2026 quantum computing roadmaps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing a closed-loop feedback system that resonantly drives the NV spin and uses a quantum-limited parametric amplifier for readout, we can actively lock the spin into a polarized state, simultaneously performing initialization and high-fidelity measurement while suppressing environmental dephasing noise.
proposals.method: We will design a microwave circuit integrating a diamond sample with a single NV center into a high-Q superconducting resonator coupled to a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) for near-quantum-limited signal amplification. A continuous microwave drive will resonantly address the NV spin transition. The JPA-amplified photocurrent or dispersive frequency shift will be fed into a fast FPGA, which will apply real-time corrective phase and amplitude adjustments to the microwave drive, implementing a resonant spin-lock feedback loop. This architecture is inspired by robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques for qudit systems and closed-loop control methods from atomic physics.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel measurement paradigm that merges initialization and readout, potentially achieving single-shot fidelity above 99%—exceeding the typical threshold for fault-tolerant measurement—without relying on optical cycling. It will provide a critical tool for all-electrical quantum processors based on diamond defects and advance methods for quantum feedback control in solid-state systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of resonant spin locking—a technique common in NMR—with quantum-limited microwave amplification and real-time feedback for a solid-state spin qubit, creating a closed-loop measurement protocol distinct from standard projective or repetitive readout schemes.

proposals.title: All-Electrical Control and Readout of Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers for Scalable Diamond Quantum Processors
proposals.motivation: The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond is a leading platform for quantum networking and sensing, but its reliance on optical initialization and readout via spin-dependent photoluminescence presents a major bottleneck for scalability and integration. Optical access requires complex free-space or integrated photonics, limiting device density and creating challenges for cryogenic operation and packaging. Developing purely electrical or magnetic control schemes would enable massively parallel, chip-scale NV-based quantum processors and memories, directly addressing a critical barrier to practical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the NV center's spin state can be polarized via electrical spin injection from a proximal ferromagnetic contact and measured through spin-dependent electrical conductivity or capacitance changes in a diamond field-effect transistor (FET) structure, bypassing the need for optical pumping and fluorescence collection.
proposals.method: We will fabricate nanoscale electronic devices on high-purity, isotopically engineered diamond substrates containing shallow NV ensembles. For initialization, we will engineer ferromagnetic (e.g., CoFe) gate electrodes to create a spin-polarized electron current into the NV defect site. For readout, we will design and characterize a diamond FET where the channel conductivity is modulated by the NV charge state (NV-/NV0), which is spin-dependent under applied microwave drives. We will employ robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, similar to those used for interacting qudit systems, to design microwave and DC pulse sequences that amplify the electrical signal and suppress noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational protocol for all-electrical operation of solid-state spin qubits, specifically for the NV center. It will demonstrate a path toward dense, optically opaque quantum processor arrays and facilitate direct integration with classical cryogenic electronics for control and readout, a key requirement for scalable quantum repeaters and computers.
proposals.novelty: While electrical readout of single charges and spins in quantum dots is established, applying this to the NV center's complex multi-level structure and achieving both initialization and readout without any optical components is a significant and unsolved challenge. Our approach uniquely combines spintronic injection with spin-to-charge conversion readout in a diamond FET, a method not yet demonstrated for high-fidelity NV spin state measurement.

proposals.title: A Robust Nuclear Spin Register as a Classical Buffer for Hybrid Quantum-Classical Processing
proposals.motivation: Hybrid quantum-classical algorithms, such as variational quantum eigensolvers, require frequent, low-latency exchange of classical data between a quantum processor and a classical computer. The bandwidth and latency of this interface, often a physical electronic link, can become a critical bottleneck. Pre-2026, diamond nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers have demonstrated exceptionally long-lived nuclear spin memories, but their use has been largely confined to storing quantum states.
proposals.hypothesis: That the robust, long-coherence-time nuclear spin register in a diamond NV center system can be repurposed as an efficient, on-chip classical data buffer, significantly reducing the communication overhead in hybrid quantum-classical computational loops.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a protocol to encode classical bit strings into the multi-level states of a register of 13C nuclear spins surrounding a single NV center electron spin. Using robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, akin to those in Lukin's qudit work, we will develop high-fidelity pulse sequences to rapidly write and read classical data via the electron spin. The system will be integrated into a control circuit where the electron spin acts as a quantum processor, and the nuclear register serves as a local memory buffer, minimizing calls to the external classical controller.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new architectural paradigm for hybrid processors, moving classical buffer functionality directly onto the quantum hardware. It will provide a concrete benchmark for the speed and fidelity of classical data handling using quantum memories, informing the design of future integrated quantum systems.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear spins are well-known as quantum memories, their systematic use as a dedicated, multi-bit classical random-access memory (RAM) buffer within a quantum processing unit is a novel and non-obvious application, blending concepts from quantum control with classical computer architecture.

proposals.title: High-Fidelity Quantum Networking via Electron-Spin-Mediated Gates in Diamond Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers
proposals.motivation: In quantum networks based on NV centers, the nuclear spin is often used as a long-lived communication qubit due to its coherence, but its slow optical and magnetic interactions limit entanglement generation rates. The electron spin offers orders-of-magnitude faster gate operations and stronger coupling to photons, but its shorter coherence time and susceptibility to noise have historically relegated it to a secondary role. This creates a critical bottleneck for scalable, high-rate quantum repeaters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing robust dynamical decoupling sequences and optimal control techniques specifically tailored for the electron spin, we can suppress its decoherence sufficiently to use it as the primary communication qubit, thereby dramatically increasing entanglement rates while preserving high fidelity.
proposals.method: We will engineer a system using individual NV centers in diamond, where the electron spin is the communication node and a nearby nuclear spin (e.g., 13C or 15N) serves as a protected memory. We will develop and implement dynamical Hamiltonian engineering protocols, inspired by recent work on interacting qudit systems, to perform high-fidelity, noise-resilient electron-spin-mediated two-qubit gates and efficient spin-photon interfaces. These protocols will be compiled into executable circuits for a programmable neutral-atom-array-like testbed, using the electron spin's fast dynamics to mediate interactions between memory qubits.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new architecture for quantum network nodes that leverages the speed of electron spins without sacrificing fidelity, directly addressing the rate-fidelity trade-off. It will provide a concrete protocol and experimental blueprint for accelerating entanglement distribution in diamond-based quantum networks, moving toward practical telecom networking with quantum memories.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious because it inverts the conventional NV center network paradigm, actively exploiting the 'noisier' electron spin as the communication workhorse rather than shielding it, by integrating advanced dynamical control typically reserved for memory qubits directly into the communication channel.

proposals.title: Experimental Realization of a Cavity-Mediated Fast Entangling Gate for Nuclear Spin Qubits in Diamond
proposals.motivation: Scalable quantum networks require efficient, high-fidelity quantum gates between long-lived memory qubits. The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center's nuclear spin offers an excellent memory, but entangling gates mediated by the electron spin are limited by its coherence time. A direct, cavity-mediated interaction between nuclear spins, as theoretically proposed, could enable faster, higher-fidelity gates, directly addressing a key bottleneck in distributed quantum computing and networking.
proposals.hypothesis: By strongly coupling a pair of NV center electron spins to a shared photonic crystal cavity mode, we can engineer an effective, fast interaction between their respective nuclear spins, enabling the deterministic generation of nuclear spin entanglement with significantly higher fidelity than electron-spin-mediated protocols.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a diamond photonic crystal cavity designed for zero-phonon-line resonance and integrate two individually addressable NV centers within the cavity mode. Using dynamic, real-time control of the electron spins via microwave and optical fields (inspired by Hamiltonian engineering techniques for qudit systems), we will implement a gate sequence that transfers the cavity-mediated electron-electron interaction to the nuclear spins. Gate performance will be characterized via quantum process tomography and Bell state fidelity measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration of a cavity-accelerated nuclear spin entangling gate, establishing a critical primitive for NV-center-based quantum processors. It will validate a key architectural path towards scalable, fault-tolerant quantum nodes with fast local operations on long-lived memories, directly impacting quantum networking and modular quantum computing.
proposals.novelty: While cavity coupling to NV electron spins has been studied, the direct application to engineer a fast, deterministic entangling gate specifically between nuclear spins—bypassing the slower electron spin coherence limit—is an unexplored experimental frontier with significant potential for performance enhancement.

proposals.title: Demonstration of a Small-Scale Quantum Algorithm on a Dynamically Reconfigurable Neutral-Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Neutral-atom arrays have emerged as a leading platform for quantum simulation and computation, with recent demonstrations of high-fidelity two-qubit gates and dynamic programmability. To progress from isolated gate demonstrations to practical quantum information processing, it is essential to integrate these gates into multi-step quantum circuits that implement non-trivial algorithms, testing the platform's coherence, control, and scalability under algorithmic load.
proposals.hypothesis: That the recently demonstrated high-fidelity Rydberg-mediated gates in dynamically field-programmable arrays can be coherently combined and sequenced to execute a small quantum algorithm with an output fidelity exceeding the threshold for quantum advantage in specific, verifiable tasks.
proposals.method: We will design and compile a 3-5 qubit quantum circuit, such as the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) or a small instance of the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) for a molecular Hamiltonian, targeting a dynamically reconfigurable neutral-atom processor. Using the band-structure engineering techniques from recent qudit work to suppress errors, we will implement the circuit by sequencing single-qubit gates and the established two-qubit Rydberg gate. Process fidelity will be characterized via quantum state tomography and cycle benchmarking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical benchmark for neutral-atom quantum processors, moving beyond component characterization to holistic circuit-level performance. It will establish a concrete workflow for algorithm compilation and execution on this platform, identifying dominant error sources in multi-step operations and informing future hardware improvements.
proposals.novelty: While two-qubit gates have been shown, their integration into a compiled, multi-step algorithm on a dynamically reconfigurable neutral-atom array, with concurrent error suppression via Hamiltonian engineering, represents a significant step toward algorithmically useful quantum circuits on this platform.

proposals.title: A Scalable Architecture for a 2D Quantum Network of Cavity-Enhanced Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers
proposals.motivation: Large-scale quantum networks require the interconnection of many high-fidelity quantum memories and processors. Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are a leading platform for quantum memory and communication, but scaling beyond single-node or small linear chains remains a critical challenge. A 2D network of coherently coupled NVs would enable multi-party entanglement distribution, fault-tolerant quantum repeaters, and distributed quantum computation, which are essential for long-range quantum communication and modular quantum computing.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating individually addressable, cavity-coupled NV centers into a 2D nanophotonic circuit, we can achieve high-fidelity, on-demand entanglement generation between any two nodes in the network, enabling scalable quantum information processing.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate a 2D network architecture based on diamond nanophotonic cavities, each containing a single NV center. The design will employ tunable directional couplers and wavelength-selective filters to route photons between nodes. We will develop compilation algorithms to map quantum circuits onto this dynamically reconfigurable 2D grid, optimizing for minimal photon loss and crosstalk. The simulation will model the performance of the full system, including the effects of cavity QED enhancement, spectral inhomogeneity of NVs, and the fidelity of Bell-state measurements for entanglement swapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed, realistic blueprint for scaling solid-state spin qubits into a 2D networked quantum processor. It will establish performance benchmarks for entanglement rates and fidelities in a large-scale NV network and identify key engineering challenges. The proposed architecture and compilation tools will directly inform the experimental development of scalable quantum repeaters and distributed quantum computers.
proposals.novelty: While 1D chains of NV centers have been explored, a comprehensive design for a fully reconfigurable 2D photonic network of cavity-coupled NVs, complete with quantum circuit compilation strategies, is novel. This approach uniquely combines Lukin group expertise in NV-cavity systems, Hamiltonian engineering for robustness, and dynamic control of neutral atom arrays, translating it to a scalable solid-state platform.

proposals.title: Demonstration of a Multi-Qudit Quantum Node via Entanglement of a Single Nitrogen-Vacancy Center with Multiple Carbon-13 Nuclear Spins
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in quantum networking and distributed quantum computation is creating multi-qubit quantum nodes with long-lived memory qubits. The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond, with its optically addressable electron spin and long-lived nuclear spin registers, is a leading candidate. However, deterministic entanglement between the NV's nitrogen nuclear spin and multiple nearby carbon-13 nuclear spins, which would form a scalable multi-qudit node, remains an outstanding experimental goal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, specifically optimized dynamical decoupling sequences and radio-frequency pulse protocols, can be used to deterministically generate and verify entanglement between the nitrogen-14 nuclear spin of a single NV center and two or more proximal carbon-13 nuclear spins.
proposals.method: We will use a single NV center in an isotopically engineered diamond sample with controlled carbon-13 concentration. The NV electron spin will be used as a bus to mediate interactions. We will design and implement multi-frequency dynamical decoupling sequences (e.g., extensions of CPMG or XY8) to selectively couple the electron spin to target nuclear spins. The 'Robust Hamiltonian Engineering' methods from Lukin's group will be applied to design robust control pulses that compensate for inhomogeneity and decoherence. Entanglement will be verified via state tomography on the constructed nuclear spin qudits.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a foundational multi-qudit quantum register within a single atomic defect, directly advancing the architecture for quantum repeaters and distributed quantum sensors. It will provide a concrete platform for testing qudit-based quantum error correction codes and algorithms, moving beyond simple two-qubit nodes.
proposals.novelty: While entanglement between an NV electron spin and single nuclei has been shown, the deterministic, simultaneous entanglement of the long-lived nitrogen nuclear spin (a qudit with spin 1) with multiple carbon-13 qubits to form a engineered multi-qudit system is a novel and non-trivial extension of existing capabilities.

proposals.title: Heralded Remote Entanglement of Nuclear Spin Qubits via Scattered Photon Detection
proposals.motivation: Scalable quantum networks require robust methods to generate entanglement between stationary matter qubits over long distances. While photon-mediated gates are promising, they often require high-fidelity photon indistinguishability and interferometric stability. An alternative, heralded approach using scattered photons can circumvent these challenges, offering a path to robust, on-demand entanglement generation for distributed quantum information processing and networking.
proposals.hypothesis: Detection of a single Raman-scattered photon from a nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond can herald the projection of its intrinsic nuclear spin and a remote, optically active spin qubit into a maximally entangled Bell state, without requiring two-photon interference.
proposals.method: We will use two NV center nodes, each with an optically addressable electron spin coupled to a long-lived nuclear spin (13C or 15N). A weak laser pulse will drive a Raman transition on each node, conditionally scattering a single photon entangled with the nuclear spin state. The photons will be collected into a telecom fiber network (leveraging Lukin group's diamond memory work) and interfered on a beam splitter. A single-photon detection event will herald the successful projection of the two remote nuclear spins into an entangled state, verified via subsequent local qubit gates and readout of the nuclear spin registers.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new protocol for deterministic, heralded remote entanglement of long-lived nuclear spin qubits, a critical resource for quantum repeaters. It will provide a robust alternative to two-photon interference schemes, advancing the toolkit for building scalable quantum networks with solid-state spin qubits.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the typical use of photon scattering for local gates, applying it instead as a heralding mechanism for remote entanglement. It uniquely combines nuclear spin memory, Raman scattering, and single-photon heralding in a solid-state architecture to bypass stringent interferometric requirements.

proposals.title: Benchmarking a Two-Photon Switch for Quantum Networks: Coherence and Fidelity in a Realistic Diamond-NV Platform
proposals.motivation: A high-fidelity, coherent two-photon switch is a critical non-linear element for scalable quantum networks and distributed quantum computing. While proof-of-principle demonstrations exist, their performance under realistic conditions—including telecom-wavelength operation, integration with quantum memories, and in the presence of noise—remains poorly characterized, hindering practical applications. This work directly addresses this gap, leveraging Lukin group expertise in diamond quantum memories and robust Hamiltonian engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing dynamical Hamiltonian engineering techniques on an interacting qudit system (specifically, a diamond NV center coupled to a nuclear spin register), we can implement a two-photon switch that maintains high quantum coherence and operational fidelity even when interfaced with a realistic telecom-band photonic channel.
proposals.method: We will construct a switch using a single NV center as the non-linear medium, with its electronic spin and nearby 13C nuclear spins forming a multi-qudit control system. The switch will be integrated with the group's demonstrated telecom-compatible quantum memory platform. We will characterize coherence via process tomography and randomized benchmarking adapted for the switch's multi-level structure. Operational fidelity will be measured by injecting weak coherent states at telecom wavelengths (via frequency conversion) and performing quantum state tomography on the output photons to directly assess the conditional quantum gate performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, application-oriented benchmark of a two-photon switch in a system directly relevant to quantum networking. It will yield concrete metrics for switch coherence and fidelity under telecom operation, identifying key limiting factors. The results will establish a critical performance roadmap for integrating non-linear photonic elements into large-scale, dynamically programmable quantum systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond idealized switch demonstrations to a system-level benchmark that combines a multi-qudit control architecture, telecom-wavelength compatibility, and rigorous quantum information protocols, all within a single, realistic experimental platform.

proposals.title: Demonstrating a Deterministic Two-Photon Controlled-Phase Gate Using Strong Rydberg Blockade in a Neutral Atom Array
proposals.motivation: The development of deterministic photon-photon interactions is a central challenge in quantum information science, enabling applications in all-optical quantum computing, quantum networking, and the generation of non-classical light. Current approaches based on weak nonlinearities in atomic ensembles or single emitters are probabilistic or require complex error correction. A deterministic multi-photon gate would represent a transformative advance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling a strongly interacting array of neutral atom qubits to a photonic mode via a high-finesse cavity, the Rydberg blockade mechanism can be engineered to mediate a deterministic, high-fidelity controlled-phase (CZ) gate between two photonic qubits encoded in the Fock states of the cavity field.
proposals.method: We will use a dynamically programmable array of 87Rb atoms as our qubit system. A subset of atoms will be coupled to a single mode of a telecom-wavelength optical cavity. We will design a multi-level excitation scheme using optimal control techniques (inspired by robust Hamiltonian engineering for qudits) to map the two-photon Fock state of the cavity mode onto a specific Rydberg-dressed state of the atomic array. The strong, long-range Rydberg interaction will induce a state-dependent phase shift only when two photons are present. The gate sequence will be compiled into a pulse sequence for the atomic array using techniques developed for field-programmable neutral atom processors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration of a deterministic two-photon quantum logic gate. It will establish a new paradigm for photonic quantum processing by directly leveraging atomic many-body interactions. The results will directly inform the design of quantum repeaters and non-linear optical elements for future quantum networks.
proposals.novelty: While Rydberg blockade is well-established for atom-atom gates, its direct application to mediate a deterministic, cavity-enhanced photon-photon gate is a novel and non-trivial extension. This approach moves beyond probabilistic schemes by exploiting the collective, non-linear response of a strongly interacting atomic array to a multi-photon field.

proposals.title: Coherent Control and Entanglement of Nuclear Spin Ensembles via Engineered Hyperfine Interactions in Neutral Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: In quantum information platforms like neutral atom arrays, the hyperfine-coupled nuclear spin bath of the host atom is typically a dominant source of decoherence for the electronic qubit. Current strategies focus on dynamical decoupling to suppress this noise. However, these nuclei represent a dense, long-lived, and naturally coupled quantum register that is largely untapped as a coherent resource for quantum memory and simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques to the electronic qubit, we can induce controlled, coherent dynamics in the surrounding nuclear spin ensemble, transforming it from a source of noise into a programmable multi-qudit quantum processor.
proposals.method: We will use a 2D array of alkali atoms (e.g., rubidium-87) with nuclear spin I=3/2 as our platform. Leveraging techniques from Lukin's recent work on robust Hamiltonian engineering for interacting qudit systems, we will design sequences of global microwave and site-specific optical Raman pulses applied to the electronic ground-state qudit. These sequences will implement dynamical nuclear spin addressing via the hyperfine interaction, enabling the compilation of quantum circuits that entangle specific electronic qubits with targeted nuclear spin sub-ensembles, and mediate nuclear-nuclear interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for quantum information processing where host nuclei are integral computational units. It will provide a protocol for creating long-lived, high-fidelity quantum memories within neutral atom arrays and establish a method for simulating complex, long-range interacting spin models using the nuclear ensemble.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear spins have been used as memory qubits in isolated systems like NV centers, the direct, programmable control and entanglement of a *dense ensemble* of nuclear spins via a *scalable array* of optically addressable electronic qubits is a novel and non-trivial extension, moving beyond noise suppression to active resource utilization.

proposals.title: Scalable Quantum Control and Entanglement of Single-Electron Spin Qubits in a Dynamically Programmable Neutral Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Two-electron spin qubits in gate-defined quantum dots or neutral atom arrays rely on exchange interactions for two-qubit gates, which are sensitive to charge noise and precise inter-qubit spacing, limiting fidelity and scalability. A simpler architecture based on robust single-electron spin qubits could circumvent these exchange-based errors, but requires new methods for high-fidelity, long-range entanglement that are compatible with scalable, dynamically reconfigurable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-fidelity, universal quantum control and entanglement of single-electron spin qubits can be achieved in a neutral atom array using global microwave fields for single-qubit gates and Rydberg-mediated interactions for two-qubit gates, thereby eliminating the need for direct exchange coupling and its associated error sources.
proposals.method: We will implement single-electron spin qubits using the ground-state valence electron of individual ytterbium-171 atoms trapped in a dynamically programmable optical tweezer array. Single-qubit gates will be performed via global microwave and Raman laser pulses addressing the hyperfine clock states. For two-qubit entangling gates, we will adapt the Rydberg blockade mechanism, coherently coupling one spin state to a Rydberg state using a two-photon transition. We will employ Hamiltonian engineering techniques, inspired by recent work on interacting qudit systems, to design robust pulse sequences that suppress errors from laser intensity fluctuations and Doppler shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a fundamentally simplified qubit node for neutral atom quantum processors, free from exchange interaction constraints. It will provide a blueprint for a scalable quantum computing architecture with reduced operational complexity and a clear path to error-corrected logical qubits using inherently robust spin qubits.
proposals.novelty: While single-electron spins are known, their integration into a scalable, dynamically programmable neutral atom processor using Rydberg-mediated entanglement for all-to-all connectivity represents a significant architectural shift from prevailing exchange-based or two-electron qubit approaches in semiconductor and atomic systems.

proposals.title: Coherent Control and Entanglement of Hole Spin Qubits in Programmable Germanium Quantum Dot Arrays
proposals.motivation: Electron spin qubits in semiconductor quantum dots face challenges from hyperfine interactions with nuclear spins, limiting coherence, and exhibit weak spin-orbit coupling, hindering long-range entanglement via photons. Hole spins in group-IV materials like germanium offer a promising alternative, with predicted strong spin-orbit coupling for fast electrical control and significantly reduced hyperfine interaction due to p-orbital character. Exploring hole spins could unlock new pathways for scalable, coherent quantum processors and hybrid quantum networking.
proposals.hypothesis: Hole spins confined in gate-defined germanium quantum dots can achieve long coherence times due to suppressed hyperfine coupling and can be manipulated via electric-dipole spin resonance enabled by strong spin-orbit interaction, allowing for high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates.
proposals.method: We will fabricate arrays of gate-defined quantum dots in strained germanium quantum wells. Using techniques from Lukin's work on dynamically field-programmable neutral atom arrays, we will design circuits and control sequences for hole spin initialization, readout via Pauli spin blockade, and coherent manipulation using microwave electric fields. We will implement robust Hamiltonian engineering methods, analogous to those used for interacting qudit systems, to calibrate and optimize exchange coupling between neighboring hole spins for two-qubit entangling gates, characterizing gate fidelity and coherence (T1, T2*).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational toolkit for hole spin qubit operation, providing the first demonstration of programmable entanglement in a germanium hole spin array. It will deliver quantitative benchmarks for hole spin coherence, gate fidelities, and spin-orbit strengths, directly informing the viability of hole-based platforms for large-scale quantum computation and their potential integration with telecom-band quantum memories.
proposals.novelty: While hole spins have been studied, a systematic approach combining high-coherence material (germaniuim), array-level programmability, and Hamiltonian engineering for robust multi-qubit control represents a significant advance beyond early single-qubit demonstrations in other systems like silicon or GaAs.

proposals.title: Dynamically Protected Two-Electron Spin Qubits in Diamond via Hamiltonian Engineering and Band-Structure Control
proposals.motivation: Long coherence times are essential for quantum information processing and simulation. For two-electron spin qubits in diamond, such as the singlet-triplet system in NV centers or divacancies, hyperfine interactions with the surrounding 13C nuclear spin bath remain a primary decoherence source, limiting coherence to the microsecond scale. Extending this coherence is critical for high-fidelity multi-qubit gates and the realization of scalable quantum networks and simulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining advanced dynamical decoupling sequences with real-time Hamiltonian engineering of the electron-nuclear interaction, we can suppress both low-frequency and resonant noise, pushing two-electron spin coherence times in diamond significantly beyond one microsecond.
proposals.method: We will implement the proposal using a dynamically field-programmable array of optically trapped neutral atoms coupled to diamond defect centers, leveraging Lukin group expertise. We will design and compile optimized quantum circuits that apply multi-axis, concatenated dynamical decoupling sequences to the two-electron spin system. Concurrently, we will use robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, informed by the local nuclear bath spectrum, to tune the qubit into a decoherence-free subspace or a band structure with suppressed hyperfine coupling, using real-time feedback from a telecom-band quantum memory node.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new benchmark for coherence times in solid-state, multi-electron spin qubits. It will provide a validated toolkit of pulse sequences and control protocols for hyperfine suppression that can be applied to other spin-defect platforms. The integration of dynamic array control with Hamiltonian engineering demonstrates a pathway toward scalable, coherent quantum processors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic co-design of compiled dynamical decoupling circuits and real-time Hamiltonian engineering specifically tailored for two-electron spin degrees of freedom, moving beyond static shielding to active, noise-adaptive protection.

proposals.title: High-Fidelity, Tunable Two-Qubit Gates via Controlled Exchange Coupling in a Silicon Quantum Dot Array
proposals.motivation: Scalable quantum computing with spin qubits in semiconductor quantum dots requires high-fidelity, fast two-qubit gates. The exchange interaction is a natural candidate for such gates, but pre-2026 demonstrations often struggle with precise, on-demand control and mitigation of noise sensitivity. Implementing a robust, calibrated exchange gate protocol is a critical step towards larger, programmable quantum dot processors.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering a dynamically controlled, pulsed exchange coupling between neighboring quantum dot spin qubits, we can realize a high-fidelity, universal two-qubit gate (specifically a sqrt(iSWAP) or controlled-phase gate) whose operation can be tuned via gate voltage sequences to compensate for quasistatic noise.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a linear array of gate-defined quantum dots in a Si/SiGe heterostructure, with each dot hosting a single electron spin qubit. Using a combination of DC gate voltages and high-bandwidth voltage pulses, we will precisely control the tunnel barrier and detuning between adjacent dots to modulate the Heisenberg exchange interaction J(t). We will implement and characterize composite pulse sequences, informed by robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques from Lukin's group's work on interacting qudit systems, to perform noise-resilient gate operations and benchmark fidelity using randomized benchmarking.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a calibrated, high-fidelity two-qubit gate primitive for silicon spin qubits, directly addressing a key bottleneck for quantum dot quantum processors. It will provide a concrete experimental framework for compiling quantum circuits into exchange-based operations, advancing the path towards dynamically programmable quantum dot arrays.
proposals.novelty: The proposal integrates advanced pulse-shaping and Hamiltonian engineering methods, typically applied to atomic and superconducting systems, directly into the control paradigm for semiconductor spin qubits to create a new standard for robust, tunable exchange gates.

proposals.title: Cross-Platform Quantum Error Correction: Implementing Trapped-Ion Surface Codes on a Dynamically Programmable Neutral Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for fault-tolerant quantum computing. While high-fidelity QEC has been demonstrated in trapped-ion systems, scaling these architectures remains challenging. Neutral atom arrays, particularly the dynamically programmable processors developed by Lukin's group, offer superior scalability and connectivity, providing an ideal testbed to implement and scale proven QEC codes.
proposals.hypothesis: Surface code protocols and Hamiltonian engineering techniques, originally developed for high-fidelity trapped-ion qubits, can be successfully compiled and executed on a neutral atom array processor to protect spin qubit information, achieving comparable or improved error suppression due to the platform's inherent connectivity and programmability.
proposals.method: We will adapt the circuit-level surface code (e.g., the [[7,1,3]] Steane code) from trapped-ion implementations for a neutral atom array. Using the group's expertise in compiling quantum circuits for dynamically field-programmable arrays, we will map the required syndrome extraction circuits onto the platform's Rydberg interaction graph. Robust Hamiltonian engineering, inspired by work on interacting qudit systems, will be employed to generate high-fidelity, parallel multi-qubit gates (CZ/CCZ) and dynamical decoupling sequences to mitigate native platform errors during extended QEC cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first implementation of a trapped-ion-derived QEC code on a neutral atom processor, directly comparing code performance across platforms. It will produce a compilation toolbox for translating QEC circuits between disparate quantum hardware, accelerating the path to fault tolerance by leveraging the best attributes of multiple qubit technologies.
proposals.novelty: The cross-platform translation of a complete QEC stack—from ion-specific protocols to a neutral atom architecture—is unexplored. The novelty lies in co-designing the error correction code with the unique capabilities (mid-circuit measurement, dynamic connectivity) of the target neutral atom system, rather than a direct port.

proposals.title: Optical Pumping of Nuclear Spin Baths for Enhanced Coherence in Diamond Quantum Processors
proposals.motivation: The coherence of electron spin qubits in solid-state systems, such as nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, is fundamentally limited by the fluctuating magnetic noise from the surrounding bath of nuclear spins (e.g., 13C). Pre-2026, dynamic decoupling and Hamiltonian engineering have been used to mitigate this noise, but achieving a high-fidelity, long-lived initialized state of the bath itself remains a significant challenge. A polarized nuclear bath would present a far quieter magnetic environment, directly extending electron spin coherence times and improving the fidelity of multi-qubit gates for quantum information processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that resonant optical pumping schemes, leveraging the NV center's spin-dependent fluorescence and existing hyperfine couplings, can be engineered to efficiently polarize the surrounding 13C nuclear spin bath into a well-defined quantum state, thereby suppressing its magnetic noise and enhancing electron spin coherence.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a protocol using a dynamically field-programmable array of NV centers. The method involves using the NV electron spin as an intermediary: first, initializing it via green laser pumping, then applying precisely sequenced microwave and radiofrequency pulses to transfer polarization to target 13C nuclei via resolved hyperfine levels. We will develop compilation algorithms to map this pumping sequence onto our neutral atom array processor architecture, enabling parallel bath initialization across multiple NV qubit nodes. The efficiency will be benchmarked via measurements of electron spin T2* and T2 echo times.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable, all-optical technique for nuclear bath initialization, directly leading to longer coherence times for NV-center qubits. It will provide a critical resource for improving the performance of diamond-based quantum memories and processors, advancing towards robust multi-node quantum networks as explored in Lukin's prior telecom networking work.
proposals.novelty: While optical pumping is standard for atomic vapors, its direct application to polarize the *disordered* nuclear spin bath in a solid-state host material is non-trivial and represents a novel synthesis of atomic physics techniques with engineered quantum control in a scalable qubit array platform.

proposals.title: Analog Quantum Simulation of Frustrated XYZ Spin Models with Dynamically Programmable Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Understanding strongly correlated quantum magnetism, especially in frustrated spin systems, remains a profound challenge for classical computation due to the exponential scaling of the Hilbert space. Analog quantum simulators, which directly emulate a target Hamiltonian, offer a promising path to study such phenomena. Pre-2026, neutral atom arrays with Rydberg interactions have demonstrated programmable Ising-type simulations, but extending this to more general XYZ models with tunable anisotropic interactions is a critical next step.
proposals.hypothesis: That a two-dimensional array of neutral atom qudits, encoded in hyperfine ground states, can be engineered via robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques to realize a fully tunable, long-range XYZ spin Hamiltonian, enabling the exploration of exotic magnetic phases and dynamics beyond the Ising limit.
proposals.method: We will utilize a dynamically field-programmable array of 87Rb atoms, where individual spins are encoded in the nuclear spin qudit manifold (e.g., F=1,2 states). Using the techniques of Hamiltonian engineering, we will design sequences of global microwave and Raman pulses to synthesize anisotropic XYZ interactions from the native van der Waals interactions between atoms excited to Rydberg states. The 'band' of available qudit states will provide a resource for suppressing unwanted terms and enhancing interaction tunability. The simulation will proceed in an analog fashion, with the circuit-like sequences defining the engineered Hamiltonian rather than digital quantum gates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, highly flexible platform for analog quantum simulation of complex magnetic models, moving beyond Ising-type interactions. It will provide direct experimental access to the phase diagram of frustrated XYZ models and their non-equilibrium dynamics, offering insights potentially relevant to quantum materials. The methodology will demonstrate how qudit encoding and Hamiltonian engineering can expand the capabilities of neutral atom arrays.
proposals.novelty: While digital quantum simulation with atoms has been proposed, this work focuses on a purely analog, Hamiltonian engineering approach using the qudit manifold for a neutral atom array to achieve continuously tunable XYZ interactions, a capability not yet demonstrated pre-2026.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Nuclear Spin Bath Dynamics via Electron Spin Coherence Mapping in Diamond NV Centers
proposals.motivation: Understanding the complex nuclear spin environment surrounding solid-state qubits is critical for improving quantum coherence and developing quantum memories. Pre-2026, methods to characterize this environment are often indirect or lack dynamical resolution. A direct, high-resolution technique to map nuclear spin dynamics would advance quantum control and sensing, enabling new applications in quantum simulation and networking.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal decay and revival structure of an electron spin's coherence under dynamical decoupling sequences encode a complete, time-resolved map of the surrounding nuclear spin bath's correlation functions and dynamics.
proposals.method: We will use a single nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond as the probe electron spin. We will implement advanced, robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, specifically optimized multi-pulse dynamical decoupling sequences (e.g., XY8, CPMG), to measure coherence decay. The inverse problem—reconstructing bath dynamics from coherence data—will be solved using a combination of compressed sensing algorithms and machine learning models trained on simulated bath configurations, leveraging the programmability of neutral atom array processors for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new protocol for non-destructive, high-resolution spectroscopy of nuclear spin environments. It will provide a direct experimental tool to validate and refine microscopic models of spin bath decoherence, directly impacting the design of robust quantum gates and long-lived quantum memories for integrated quantum systems.
proposals.novelty: While sensing static nuclear configurations is established, our approach focuses on inverting coherence data to reconstruct *time-dependent* bath correlation functions, moving beyond spectral characterization to dynamical mapping, which is a significantly more complex inverse problem.

proposals.title: Demonstration of Dynamically Programmable Surface Code Operations on a Neutral-Atom Qubit Array
proposals.motivation: Neutral-atom arrays have emerged as a leading platform for quantum simulation and computation, offering programmable qubit geometries and high-fidelity gates. However, achieving fault-tolerant quantum computation requires integrating these capabilities with quantum error correction (QEC) codes. As of 2025, while topological and stabilizer codes like the surface code have been implemented in superconducting and trapped-ion systems, their adaptation to the unique dynamical control and connectivity of neutral-atom processors remains a critical, open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That the dynamically field-programmable nature of neutral-atom arrays, combined with robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, can be leveraged to implement the syndrome extraction circuits of a surface code efficiently, achieving logical error rates below the physical error rate threshold.
proposals.method: We will design and compile quantum circuits for a distance-3 surface code onto a 2D array of Rydberg atom qubits, utilizing the dynamically reconfigurable connectivity of the platform. The syndrome extraction cycles will be implemented using a combination of globally driven, robust multi-qubit gates (inspired by qudit control methods) and mid-circuit measurement and reset. We will employ the band-structure engineering techniques from Lukin's group to create protected interaction manifolds for the ancilla qubits during parity-check operations, mitigating coherent errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimental blueprint for executing a topological QEC code on a neutral-atom processor, moving the platform from NISQ-era demonstrations toward fault tolerance. It will establish a concrete compilation framework mapping stabilizer circuits to the specific constraints and strengths of atomic arrays, including dynamic connectivity and global addressing.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a dynamically programmable qubit array with the rigid geometric requirements of the surface code is non-trivial. The novelty lies in co-designing the QEC circuit compilation and the robust Hamiltonian engineering pulses specifically for neutral atoms, rather than directly porting architectures from other platforms.

proposals.title: Inverse Hamiltonian Engineering: Probing Rydberg Potentials and Stray Fields via High-Fidelity Gate Tomography
proposals.motivation: High-fidelity quantum gates in neutral atom arrays are critically sensitive to the precise form of Rydberg interaction potentials and environmental stray fields. Current characterization methods, like direct spectroscopy, are often indirect or perturbative. A method to use the gate itself as a primary diagnostic tool would provide a direct, in-situ probe of the fundamental interactions limiting processor performance, enabling better engineering and error suppression.
proposals.hypothesis: The measured fidelity of a dynamically engineered multi-qubit gate (e.g., a CZ or CPHASE gate) is a highly sensitive and information-rich observable that can be inverted to reconstruct key parameters of the underlying Rydberg interaction potential and map spatially varying stray electric and magnetic fields across the array.
proposals.method: We will implement robust Hamiltonian engineering, as demonstrated in Lukin's group for qudit systems, to create a family of high-fidelity entangling gates on a programmable neutral atom array (e.g., using a 'dynamically field-programmable' architecture). We will then perform gate tomography across different atom pairs and geometries. An inverse optimization algorithm, likely a Bayesian inference or variational approach, will be developed to fit the observed fidelity data to a parameterized model of the Rydberg potential (including beyond-blockade terms) and local Stark/Zeeman shifts, using the known gate sequence as the forward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel metrological technique for quantum processor characterization, moving from diagnosing average error rates to reconstructing specific underlying physical perturbations. It will provide direct experimental validation of Rydberg interaction models at the atomic scale and generate calibrated spatial maps of stray fields, informing hardware improvements for scaling.
proposals.novelty: While gate fidelity is commonly used as a performance metric, its systematic inversion to reconstruct Hamiltonian parameters is a non-obvious application of quantum control, transforming a diagnostic outcome into a precise measurement protocol. This approach is distinct from prior spectroscopic methods by being native to the processor's operational mode.

proposals.title: Generalized Dark-State Polaritons in Multi-Level Atomic Systems for Robust Quantum Networking
proposals.motivation: The dark-state polariton (DSP) formalism, central to electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) and quantum memory, has been predominantly developed for simple three-level Lambda systems. However, advanced quantum networking applications, such as multi-mode memory, frequency conversion, and interfacing disparate qubits, require more complex atomic level schemes (e.g., tripod, N-level, or diamond configurations). A generalized theory is needed to harness the full potential of multi-level atoms, like those in alkaline-earth or Rydberg arrays, for robust quantum information processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic generalization of the DSP formalism to complex, multi-level atomic schemes will reveal new classes of protected photonic modes and enable deterministic engineering of light-matter interactions for applications beyond simple storage and retrieval.
proposals.method: We will employ a Hamiltonian engineering approach, extending techniques from Lukin's work on interacting qudit systems. The method involves: 1) Deriving the general dark-state subspace conditions for N-level systems with multiple control fields using Lie algebra techniques. 2) Developing numerical algorithms to compile optimal control pulse sequences that adiabatically manipulate these generalized polaritons. 3) Applying the theory to specific architectures, such as neutral atom arrays with Rydberg-dressed states and telecom-compatible diamond quantum memories with complex spin manifolds, to design novel quantum circuits and protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical framework for quantum optics in multi-level systems, directly enabling the design of high-fidelity, multi-mode quantum memories and deterministic photonic gates. It will establish new protocols for robust long-distance quantum networking by facilitating efficient frequency conversion and interconnects between different physical qubit platforms.
proposals.novelty: While DSPs are well-understood for Lambda systems, a comprehensive theory for arbitrary multi-level configurations—particularly those with degenerate states or multiple excited levels—is lacking. The novelty lies in combining this generalized formalism with modern Hamiltonian engineering and quantum circuit compilation techniques for practical device design.

proposals.title: Deterministic Quantum Gate Operations and Entanglement Distribution via Dark-State Polaritons in a Programmable Neutral Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Robust, long-range quantum links between stationary qubits are a central challenge for scalable quantum information processing. Dark-state polaritons (DSPs), which are hybrid light-matter excitations, offer a promising mechanism for coherent quantum state transfer, as demonstrated in atomic ensembles and quantum memories. However, their integration into a programmable, gate-based quantum processor for deterministic entanglement and computation remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DSPs, generated via electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) in a neutral atom array, can be used to mediate deterministic, high-fidelity entangling gates between distant qubits and enable efficient entanglement distribution across the processor.
proposals.method: We will implement this using a dynamically field-programmable array of neutral atom qubits (e.g., Rubidium-87). A control atom will be coupled to a photonic mode in a nanophotonic structure to generate a DSP. Using robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques, we will design pulse sequences to map the atomic state onto the DSP, propagate it, and map it onto a target atom. This protocol will be compiled into quantum circuits for the neutral atom processor to realize a controlled-phase gate between non-local qubits and create Bell pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, hardware-efficient protocol for long-range interactions in neutral atom quantum computers, moving beyond Rydberg blockade. It will demonstrate a hybrid quantum node architecture compatible with telecom networking, directly linking Lukin group's expertise in neutral atom arrays, diamond memories, and Hamiltonian engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deterministic use of DSPs for gate operations within a gate-model quantum circuit, rather than solely for memory or probabilistic transfer, and its integration into a scalable, programmable neutral atom system.

proposals.title: Real-Time Quantum Feedback Control of Rydberg Polariton Propagation in a Dynamically Programmable Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Rydberg polaritons, which combine photonic coherence with strong atomic interactions, are a leading platform for quantum networking and simulation. However, their propagation fidelity is highly sensitive to noise in the control field, limiting their utility in scalable quantum circuits and long-distance telecom links. Stabilizing these excitations in real-time is a critical, unsolved challenge for robust quantum information processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing real-time quantum feedback, where the optical control field is dynamically adjusted based on continuous weak measurement of the polariton state, will significantly suppress decoherence and propagation errors, enabling high-fidelity polariton transport in noisy environments.
proposals.method: We will implement this using a dynamically field-programmable array of neutral atom qubits, where Rydberg polaritons are generated via electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT). A weak off-resonant probe will perform continuous, non-destructive measurement of the polariton propagation. This signal will feed into a classical FPGA-based controller running a Kalman filter or PID algorithm to compute corrections, which are applied in real-time via a high-bandwidth acousto-optic modulator (AOM) controlling the EIT coupling field. The system's programmability allows us to test this feedback on various circuit geometries and noise models.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first integration of real-time optical feedback control with Rydberg polariton systems, directly extending the concept of robust Hamiltonian engineering to dynamic error correction. It will provide a critical tool for stabilizing quantum states in neutral atom quantum processors and quantum memories, advancing towards fault-tolerant quantum networking.
proposals.novelty: While feedback control is established in quantum optics, its application to stabilize the *propagation* of strongly interacting Rydberg polaritons in a programmable atom array is novel, merging concepts from quantum circuit compilation, Hamiltonian engineering, and quantum measurement in a new way.

proposals.title: Chip-Scale Quantum Memory and Photonic Gates via Dark-State Polaritons in Integrated Rare-Earth-Doped Waveguides
proposals.motivation: Dark-state polaritons, hybrid light-matter excitations, enable robust quantum memory and nonlinear photonic gates, but demonstrations have been confined to bulk atomic ensembles. To enable scalable quantum networks and photonic quantum information processing, these powerful phenomena must be miniaturized onto a chip. Integrated photonic platforms, particularly rare-earth-doped waveguides offering long optical coherence times, provide a promising but unexplored path to achieve this.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the optical depth and collective coupling in a rare-earth-ion-doped waveguide array, we can generate and coherently control dark-state polaritons, thereby implementing a deterministic, on-chip quantum memory and programmable photon-photon interaction gate.
proposals.method: We will fabricate high-Q waveguides in yttrium orthosilicate (YSO) doped with neodymium ions (Nd³⁺) using focused ion beam milling. We will design and implement dynamic control sequences, inspired by robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques for qudit systems, to shape the optical fields and create the electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) condition. Using a dynamically programmable optical setup, we will map photonic qubits encoded in telecom-wavelength pulses onto collective atomic excitations (dark-state polaritons), store them, and retrieve them on demand, characterizing fidelity and efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first integrated photonic platform for dark-state polariton physics, moving quantum memory from a table-top component to a scalable, on-chip device. It will establish a foundational element for future chip-scale quantum repeaters and enable new architectures for photonic quantum circuits with built-in memory and nonlinearity.
proposals.novelty: While EIT and polaritons are well-known in atomic physics, their implementation in a solid-state, waveguide-integrated platform with engineered collective states represents a significant and non-trivial advance in quantum photonic hardware, merging atomic quantum memory concepts with scalable integrated photonics.

proposals.title: High-Efficiency Entanglement Purification for Quantum Networks Based on Atomic Ensembles and Linear Optics
proposals.motivation: Scalable quantum networks require high-fidelity, long-distance entanglement, but transmission through lossy channels degrades fidelity. Entanglement purification is essential to distill high-quality Bell pairs from noisy ones. Current protocols, often designed for abstract qubit models, are inefficient for the specific noise characteristics and operational constraints of leading physical platforms like atomic ensemble memories coupled with linear optics.
proposals.hypothesis: By tailoring entanglement purification circuits and classical processing to the dominant error modes and native gate sets of atomic ensemble and linear optics systems, we can achieve significantly higher purification yield and final fidelity compared to generic, platform-agnostic protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a compilation framework that maps abstract purification circuits (e.g., DEJMPS, hashing) onto the native operations of the Lukin group's platform: atomic ensemble qubits, Rydberg-mediated gates, and linear-optics-based Bell measurements.
