Using pre-2026 Hamiltonian engineering techniques for robustness, we will design optimized multi-qubit control sequences. We will then employ numerical simulation and convex optimization to tailor the protocol parameters (e.g., measurement bases, acceptance probabilities) to the platform's specific non-Pauli noise model, minimizing resource overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a set of concrete, executable purification circuits and associated classical control logic for atomic ensemble systems, directly boosting the performance of quantum repeater nodes. It will establish a methodology for co-designing quantum error correction protocols with physical hardware, moving beyond the abstract circuit model.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the hardware-in-the-loop optimization of purification, moving from generic qubit protocols to ones that exploit the specific coherence properties, collective operations, and measurement imperfections of atomic ensembles and linear optics, an approach not fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Co-Propagation of Quantum and Classical Signals in a Diamond-Memory-Based Quantum Repeater Node on Deployed Telecom Fiber
proposals.motivation: Long-distance quantum communication via entanglement distribution requires quantum repeaters. While atomic ensemble memories, like those based on diamond silicon-vacancy (SiV) centers, offer a promising platform, their integration into existing telecom fiber infrastructure remains a critical, unsolved engineering challenge. Demonstrating robust co-propagation of quantum signals with classical telecom traffic on the same fiber is essential for a practical, cost-effective quantum network.
proposals.hypothesis: That a diamond SiV-center quantum memory node can be interfaced with standard, in-use telecom fiber to demonstrate entanglement heralding or spin-photon entanglement, while simultaneously carrying classical data channels, by employing specific frequency and temporal multiplexing protocols to mitigate noise.
proposals.method: We will construct a quantum repeater node based on a diamond SiV ensemble memory, employing robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques to optimize optical transitions for the telecom C-band. The node will be connected to a local fiber loop emulating a 20-40 km span of deployed fiber carrying dense wavelength-division multiplexed (DWDM) classical signals. We will develop dynamic filtering circuits and temporal gating protocols to isolate the quantum band (e.g., 1550 nm) from amplified spontaneous emission noise and Raman scatter from classical pumps. The system's performance will be quantified by measuring the fidelity of stored spin-photon entanglement before and after integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental blueprint for interfacing an atomic-ensemble quantum repeater with active telecom infrastructure, quantifying the specific noise budgets and necessary isolation techniques. It will move quantum repeater research from controlled lab environments toward real-world deployment scenarios, directly addressing a major bottleneck for the quantum internet.
proposals.novelty: Previous work on diamond quantum memories has focused on standalone performance or links with dark fibers. The novelty here is the explicit engineering of the quantum-classical interface within a single fiber, leveraging the diamond system's inherent coherence and the team's expertise in Hamiltonian engineering to solve a specific, systems-level integration problem.

proposals.title: Fault-Tolerant Quantum Networking: Integrating Dynamically Encoded Photonic Qubits with Diamond Quantum Memories
proposals.motivation: Long-distance quantum communication is hindered by photon loss and operational errors, which degrade entanglement fidelity and limit the scalability of quantum networks. While diamond-based quantum memories, as demonstrated in Lukin's group, provide a promising stationary qubit platform, the transmitted photonic qubits remain vulnerable. Applying quantum error correction (QEC) directly to these flying qubits is a critical, unresolved challenge for building robust, large-scale quantum networks.
proposals.hypothesis: By dynamically encoding photonic qubits into small-scale bosonic or repetition codes at the network node level, the logical information can be protected during transmission and faithfully transferred to and from diamond spin qubit memories, significantly enhancing end-to-end entanglement fidelity compared to unprotected transmission.
proposals.method: We propose to design a hybrid node architecture combining a dynamically field-programmable neutral atom array (as a source/processor) with a diamond quantum memory. The neutral atom array will generate photonic graph states and perform real-time encoding of quantum information into a photonic [[7,1,3]] Steane code or a bosonic cat code using active feedback control. Error-corrected photonic states will be transmitted over a telecom link and mapped onto the robust, long-lived spin states of a nitrogen-vacancy center in diamond. The circuit compilation techniques from Lukin's recent work will be adapted to optimize the encoding/decoding sequences and the interface gates between the photonic channel and the memory qubit.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a key enabling protocol for fault-tolerant quantum repeaters by providing the first experimental blueprint for integrating active QEC with long-lived quantum memories in a network node. It will establish a concrete performance benchmark for logical error rates in a real-world quantum link, moving beyond proof-of-principle memory demonstrations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of dynamical quantum error correction circuits for flying qubits with the specific Hamiltonian engineering and control protocols needed for robust interfacing with diamond qudit systems, creating an integrated correction-and-memory pipeline rather than treating these functions separately.

proposals.title: Integrated Photonic-Chip Platform for Miniaturized Atomic Ensemble Quantum Nodes
proposals.motivation: Atomic ensembles, particularly in diamond (NV centers) and alkali vapors, are leading platforms for quantum memories and repeaters, essential for long-distance quantum networks. However, current implementations rely on bulky, table-top optics and vacuum systems, which severely limits scalability and practical deployment. A chip-scale, integrated photonic platform for atomic ensembles is a critical missing technology for moving quantum networks from the lab to real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging integrated photonics and nanofabrication techniques, we can create a compact, monolithic chip that co-integrates a microscopic atomic ensemble (e.g., a diamond waveguide with NV centers or a micro-cell with alkali vapor) with necessary optical circuits for state preparation, manipulation, and telecom-wavelength conversion, without sacrificing the ensemble's coherence properties or light-matter coupling efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a hybrid silicon nitride (SiN) photonic circuit on a diamond substrate containing a shallow layer of NV centers. The circuit will incorporate grating couplers for external laser addressing, microring resonators to enhance interaction with the ensemble, and electro-optic modulators for fast control. A key element will be the integration of a nonlinear wavelength converter, based on periodically poled waveguides, to interface the NV emission band (637 nm) with the telecom C-band (1550 nm). We will adapt Hamiltonian engineering techniques from Lukin's group to design robust control pulses for the integrated qudit systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully integrated photonic chip containing a functional atomic ensemble quantum memory node with telecom interface. It will demonstrate a drastic reduction in the physical footprint and complexity of such nodes. The results will provide a clear pathway towards manufacturable, arrayed quantum network components, directly impacting the fields of quantum communication and distributed quantum computation.
proposals.novelty: While integrated photonics for single emitters (e.g., quantum dots) and for atomic vapor cells exists, the monolithic co-integration of a solid-state atomic ensemble (NV centers in diamond) with all necessary pump, control, and frequency conversion optics on a single chip is unexplored. This approach moves beyond hybrid assembly to a truly scalable, wafer-level fabrication process for ensemble-based nodes.

proposals.title: A Robust, Dynamically Reconfigurable Metropolitan Quantum Network Node Based on Diamond Quantum Memories and Programmable Neutral Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: While long-distance quantum repeater networks are a long-term goal, demonstrating robust, functional quantum links over metropolitan distances (10-50 km) is a critical near-term milestone. Such a network would enable the first practical applications of distributed quantum computing and secure communication between urban data centers. Current demonstrations lack the robustness, scalability, and dynamic reconfigurability needed for a real-world, multi-user network.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a telecom-compatible diamond quantum memory as a robust network interface with a dynamically programmable neutral atom array processor as a local quantum resource, we can construct a metropolitan network node capable of on-demand entanglement distribution and remote quantum operations.
proposals.method: We will engineer a network node architecture where a single NV center in diamond, with spin-photon entanglement at telecom wavelengths, serves as the communication qubit. This will be interfaced, via local optical links and quantum gates, with a small-scale array of neutral atom qubits manipulated using dynamically field-programmable gates. We will employ robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques to protect the memory qubit and the processor qubits from noise during network operations. Custom quantum circuits will be compiled to manage entanglement purification protocols across the node and perform remote gate operations between separate nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a blueprint and experimental demonstration of a key building block for a metropolitan quantum internet, moving beyond point-to-point links to a functional network node. It will show how to integrate disparate quantum technologies (solid-state memories and neutral atom processors) into a cohesive system. The results will provide critical data on the fidelity and rate of entanglement distribution achievable in a real urban fiber environment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a robust quantum memory for networking with a fully programmable local quantum processor, enabling the node to not just store and forward quantum states, but also to process them on-demand. This dynamic functionality, tailored for the metropolitan scale, goes beyond static network demonstrations.

proposals.title: Distributed Quantum Sensing of Optical Media via Protocol Loss Tomography
proposals.motivation: Quantum communication protocols, such as entanglement distribution, are highly sensitive to channel loss, which is typically viewed as a detrimental noise source. However, this sensitivity represents a powerful, untapped resource for probing the physical properties of the transmission medium itself. A method to invert this relationship—using a quantum protocol as a sensor for spatially-resolved loss profiles—would open new applications in non-invasive material characterization and distributed environmental monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering multi-qubit probe states and sequences of local gates on a programmable neutral atom array, and by measuring the fidelity of distributed quantum states after transmission, we can reconstruct a spatial loss map of an intervening optical channel with precision surpassing classical intensity-based methods.
proposals.method: We will employ a dynamically field-programmable array of neutral atom qubits to generate multi-partite entangled probe states, such as graph states, optimized for loss discrimination. These states will be transmitted through a channel under test to a distant diamond quantum memory node, acting as a heralded receiver. Using techniques from robust Hamiltonian engineering and compiling adaptive quantum circuits based on prior measurement outcomes, we will implement a Bayesian estimation algorithm to iteratively reconstruct the channel's attenuation profile along multiple spatial or spectral modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of 'protocol tomography,' where a quantum information protocol is repurposed as a high-performance sensor. It will provide a concrete method for distributed quantum sensing of optical materials and free-space links, directly leveraging advances in quantum networking hardware. The results will bridge the fields of quantum communication and metrology.
proposals.novelty: While loss characterization is classical, using the *functional dependence* of a complex multi-qubit quantum protocol's outcome on distributed channel loss as the sensing mechanism is novel. This approach moves beyond using simple photon counting or heralded pairs to employing the computational resources of entangled qubit arrays for superior spatial resolution and discrimination.

proposals.title: Feedback-Compensated Engineered Dissipation for Open Quantum Many-Body Systems with Programmable Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Engineered dissipation is a powerful tool for stabilizing quantum phases and preparing entangled states, but its experimental implementation in many-body systems is plagued by uncontrolled, inhomogeneous background particle loss. This technical noise obscures the intrinsic open-system dynamics, limiting the reproducibility and fundamental understanding of dissipative many-body phenomena. A method to characterize and actively compensate for this loss is therefore critical for advancing the field of open quantum matter.
proposals.hypothesis: That a real-time feedback loop, using site-resolved loss measurements to programmatically adjust optical potentials, can homogenize and precisely define the dissipative environment in a neutral atom array, thereby enabling the study of pure, engineered open quantum dynamics.
proposals.method: We will use a high-resolution quantum gas microscope for non-destructive, site-resolved parity measurements to construct a spatial map of background loss rates across a 2D array of rubidium-87 atoms. This map will serve as input to a real-time feedback controller that modulates the intensity of a digital micromirror device (DMD), applying compensatory site-dependent potentials to counteract loss inhomogeneity. For active dissipation engineering, we will use the same DMD to dynamically shape the regions addressed by a global optical pumping beam, implementing uniform, programmable dissipative 'sinks' as described in robust Hamiltonian engineering protocols for qudit systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel experimental platform where the dissipative landscape is a precisely characterized and tunable parameter. It will enable the first unambiguous studies of dissipative phase transitions and non-equilibrium steady states in many-body systems, turning a major source of technical noise into a controlled resource. The compiled feedback circuits will establish a new paradigm for error-corrected quantum simulation in open systems.
proposals.novelty: While quantum gas microscopy and programmable potentials are established, their integration into a real-time feedback loop specifically to characterize and compensate for background loss—transforming it from a bug into a feature—is a novel and non-obvious synthesis for open quantum systems research.

proposals.title: Engineering Dissipative Phases and Dynamics in a Programmable Rydberg Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Understanding open quantum many-body systems, where dissipation and interactions compete, is a frontier challenge in quantum science. Pre-2026, analog quantum simulators have excelled at studying closed-system equilibrium physics, but a general platform for probing driven-dissipative quantum matter with high programmability is lacking. This work would open a new experimental direction for exploring non-equilibrium phases and quantum information dynamics in the presence of engineered loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing site-resolved, controlled particle loss into a programmable Rydberg atom array, we can stabilize novel dissipative many-body phases and observe distinct dynamical regimes, such as dissipative phase transitions and anomalous relaxation, not accessible in closed systems.
proposals.method: We will use a dynamically field-programmable neutral atom array (based on Lukin group's recent work) of Rb-87 atoms as our quantum simulator. Controlled, site-selective dissipation will be implemented via a resonant excitation beam that incoherently removes atoms (e.g., to a short-lived excited state) at programmable rates, acting as a particle loss channel. We will combine this with robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques (e.g., using optimal control for interacting qudit systems) to create and probe models like the dissipative Bose-Hubbard or XYZ spin model. Dynamics will be tracked via site-resolved fluorescence imaging and correlation measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental toolkit for open quantum system simulation with neutral atoms, directly probing the interplay of coherent interactions and engineered dissipation. It will provide some of the first microscopic observations of dissipative phase transitions and non-equilibrium steady states in a tunable, two-dimensional quantum system, offering insights for quantum metrology and error correction.
proposals.novelty: While dissipation is often considered a nuisance, this proposal actively exploits it as a programmable resource in a highly coherent quantum simulator, moving beyond simple global loss to site- and state-resolved dissipation schemes integrated with complex Hamiltonian engineering.

proposals.title: Robust Hamiltonian Engineering for Enhanced Coherence and Programmable Interactions in Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Neutral atom arrays with Rydberg interactions are a leading platform for quantum simulation and computation, but gate fidelities and coherence are limited by imperfect Rydberg excitation control and sensitivity to environmental noise. Enhancing coherence and enabling complex, programmable interaction sequences is critical for scaling to larger, more useful quantum systems. This directly addresses a core bottleneck in realizing practical quantum applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying techniques from robust Hamiltonian engineering and optimal control theory to the Rydberg excitation process, we can significantly suppress decoherence from laser noise, Doppler shifts, and inhomogeneous broadening, thereby extending coherence times and enabling high-fidelity, dynamically programmable interaction sequences.
proposals.method: We will implement a combination of composite pulse sequences and adiabatic rapid passage protocols, optimized using gradient ascent pulse engineering (GRAPE) algorithms, to shape the excitation lasers. This will be tested on a dynamically field-programmable neutral atom array processor. We will benchmark performance by measuring coherence times of collective Rydberg states and fidelity of multi-qubit gates and interaction sequences, comparing them to standard square-pulse excitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a toolbox of robust control methods for Rydberg systems, directly leading to higher-fidelity quantum gates and longer-lived entangled states. It will enable the execution of more complex quantum circuits and simulation protocols that require precise, time-dependent Hamiltonian control, advancing the capabilities of neutral atom quantum processors.
proposals.novelty: While robust control is well-studied in NMR and trapped ions, its systematic application to the specific challenges of Rydberg excitation in large, programmable neutral atom arrays—particularly for generating complex, time-dependent interaction graphs—remains largely unexplored and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Error-Mitigated Quantum Simulation and Optimization with Dynamically Programmable Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Neutral atom arrays with Rydberg interactions are a leading platform for analog quantum simulation and combinatorial optimization. However, the accuracy of these analog quantum processors is limited by coherent errors from imperfect pulse control and environmental noise. Adapting digital error mitigation techniques could significantly enhance result fidelity without the overhead of full quantum error correction, which is currently infeasible.
proposals.hypothesis: That coherent error channels in analog Rydberg quantum simulators can be systematically characterized and mitigated using extrapolation techniques adapted from digital quantum computing, leading to measurable improvements in the accuracy of measured observables.
proposals.method: We will implement Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE) on a dynamically field-programmable neutral atom array processor. By intentionally scaling the amplitude or duration of the global Rabi frequency and detuning pulses (the primary error sources), we will generate a family of expectation values for observables like spin-spin correlations. These will be extrapolated to the zero-error limit using Richardson and exponential fitting. The protocol will be tested on Ising Hamiltonian dynamics and Max-Cut problem instances, leveraging the group's expertise in robust Hamiltonian engineering and compilation for these systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a practical, software-level error mitigation framework for analog quantum simulators, directly increasing their utility for near-term quantum advantage experiments. It will provide a quantitative benchmark for coherent error impact and a pathway to more accurate simulations of quantum magnetism and approximate optimization.
proposals.novelty: While ZNE is established for digital gate-based circuits, its application to the continuous-time, analog evolution of Rydberg atom arrays—where errors are inherently non-local and Hamiltonian-specific—is unexplored and non-trivial, requiring new models for error scaling.

proposals.title: Direct Observation of Vison Dynamics and Confinement via a Gauged Dual Quantum Simulation of the 2D Transverse-Field Ising Model
proposals.motivation: Topological excitations like visons in quantum spin systems are fundamental to exotic phases like quantum spin liquids, but their direct experimental detection remains a major challenge. Duality transformations, such as mapping the 2D quantum Ising model to a Z2 lattice gauge theory, formally recast spin correlations into gauge-invariant Wilson loop observables, but this mapping has rarely been exploited as a practical measurement protocol in quantum simulators. Implementing this dual picture explicitly could unlock new spectroscopic signatures of confinement and deconfinement transitions that are otherwise obscured in the original spin basis.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering the dual Z2 gauge theory of the 2D transverse-field Ising model on a programmable quantum simulator, the dynamical correlation functions of Wilson loops will directly reveal the spectral weight and confinement dynamics of vison excitations, providing a clear experimental signature of the topological phase transition.
proposals.method: We will implement the dual theory using a neutral atom array quantum processor, encoding the gauge field spins on the links of a square lattice. Using the platform's dynamic programmability for Rydberg interactions and local addressing, we will construct the gauge-invariant ring-exchange Hamiltonian and implement local 'electric' and 'magnetic' field terms. We will develop quantum circuits to prepare ground states across the phase diagram and measure real-time Wilson loop correlators via non-destructive parity measurements on atom subsets, enabled by the system's single-shot readout. Numerical simulations of the circuits using tensor network methods will benchmark the experimental protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete protocol for measuring topological excitations in quantum simulators via a duality-based measurement framework. It will provide the first direct experimental data on vison dynamics and their confinement transition in a tunable 2D system, offering a new paradigm for probing topological order beyond static entanglement measures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using quantum simulation not to emulate the original spin model, but to directly engineer its dual gauge theory as the primary physical system, thereby turning a formal mathematical duality into an engineered measurement tool for extracting otherwise inaccessible dynamical spectral functions.

proposals.title: Quantum Simulation of the Dual Ising Model with Dynamically Programmable Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: The Ising model is a cornerstone of statistical mechanics and quantum magnetism, but its dual representation, which maps local operators to non-local strings, offers a complementary perspective on phase transitions and excitations. Exploring this duality on a quantum simulator could reveal new insights into topological order and confinement-deconfinement transitions, phenomena central to understanding exotic quantum materials. However, a direct experimental implementation of the dual model's non-local interactions has remained a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transverse-field Ising model and its exact dual can be engineered on the same programmable Rydberg atom array processor by applying a combination of local qubit rotations and non-local entangling gates, enabling the observation of dual physics such as the proliferation of topological defects.
proposals.method: We will use a dynamically field-programmable array of neutral atoms (e.g., Rubidium-87) as interacting Rydberg qubits. The primal Ising model will be implemented via well-established Rydberg blockade mechanisms. To realize the dual model, we will compile a quantum circuit that performs a Kramers-Wannier duality transformation on the hardware, mapping local spin-flip operators to string operators. This will involve sequences of controlled-phase gates and single-qubit rotations, leveraging recent advances in robust Hamiltonian engineering for multi-level qudit systems to enhance gate fidelity and suppress errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first quantum simulation of the exact dual of a canonical spin model, providing a new tool to probe non-local order parameters and topological excitations. It will establish a methodology for implementing duality transformations on programmable quantum processors, opening a path to study dualities in more complex models relevant to high-energy physics and condensed matter theory.
proposals.novelty: While duality is a well-known theoretical concept, its active compilation and execution as a quantum circuit on a neutral-atom processor to compare primal and dual dynamics is a novel experimental paradigm. This approach moves beyond analog simulation of a fixed Hamiltonian to a digitally-assisted exploration of dual spaces.

proposals.title: Quantum Simulation and Computation with Tunable Dipole-Dipole Interactions in Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Rydberg atom arrays have emerged as a leading platform for quantum simulation and computation, primarily leveraging the Rydberg blockade regime where strong dipole interactions prevent multiple excitations. However, many interesting quantum many-body models and gate schemes require intermediate interaction strengths. The regime of weak or tunable dipole interactions, which breaks perfect blockade, remains largely unexplored but is crucial for simulating exotic quantum phases and enabling new computational primitives.
proposals.hypothesis: By precisely engineering weak or tunable dipole-dipole interactions in neutral atom arrays, we can access novel quantum many-body phases and implement high-fidelity multi-qubit gates that are not possible under perfect blockade, thereby expanding the computational power of the platform.
proposals.method: We will use dynamically field-programmable neutral atom arrays with individually addressable Rydberg states. By tuning the principal quantum number, electric fields, and inter-atomic distances, we will create a spatially and temporally programmable interaction profile. We will develop compilation algorithms for quantum circuits that account for these non-blockade interactions, and employ Hamiltonian engineering techniques, inspired by recent qudit work, to design effective dynamics and gates resilient to the resulting coherent errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new operational paradigm for neutral atom quantum processors, moving beyond strict blockade. It will deliver a toolbox for simulating quantum models with tunable long-range interactions and demonstrate novel multi-qubit entangling gates, directly impacting quantum simulation and circuit-based quantum computation.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on maximizing blockade strength, we deliberately operate in the non-blockade regime, treating the tunable interaction as a resource rather than an imperfection, and develop systematic control and compilation methods to harness it.

proposals.title: High-Fidelity, Dynamically Reconfigurable Two-Qubit Gates via Optimized Dipole Blockade in a Programmable Neutral Atom Array
proposals.motivation: Neutral atom arrays with Rydberg-mediated dipole blockade are a leading platform for quantum simulation and computation, but achieving high-fidelity, scalable two-qubit gates remains a critical challenge. Current gate designs often suffer from errors from imperfect blockade, atomic motion, and residual interactions, limiting the depth of executable quantum circuits. A concrete, optimized gate architecture is needed to unlock practical applications in quantum algorithms and error correction.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining robust Hamiltonian engineering techniques with dynamic, site-specific pulse shaping, we can design and implement a family of CZ and CNOT gate protocols that systematically suppress coherent errors and exceed 99.5% fidelity in a field-programmable atom array.
proposals.method: We will design gate protocols using optimal control theory (e.g., GRAPE) to shape excitation pulses for individual qubits in a dynamically programmable array, directly leveraging Lukin group's recent work on compiling for such processors. The core architecture will employ the Rydberg dipole blockade, but we will engineer multi-level 'qudit' dynamics to suppress leakage and phase errors, as inspired by their work on robust Hamiltonian engineering. Experimental realization will use a rubidium-87 array, with gates characterized via randomized benchmarking and state tomography.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete, high-performance gate blueprint for neutral atom quantum processors, moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations. It will provide a critical component for executing deeper quantum circuits and algorithms on this scalable platform. The results will directly inform compiler design for field-programmable arrays, bridging the gap between hardware and algorithms.
proposals.novelty: The integration of dynamic, site-specific pulse optimization with Hamiltonian engineering for error suppression in a fully programmable array is a non-obvious advance over static, global-pulse gate schemes. It tailors the gate design to the unique capabilities and constraints of the latest generation of neutral atom processors.

proposals.title: Quantum Trajectory Analysis of Measurement-Induced Phase Transitions in Programmable Rydberg Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: Recent experiments with programmable Rydberg atom arrays have demonstrated the ability to prepare and probe many-body quantum states, including those with volume-law entanglement. A key open question is understanding the role of measurement back-action and open-system dynamics in such platforms, particularly in the context of measurement-induced phase transitions (MIPTs). Pre-2026, direct experimental observation of MIPTs in large-scale quantum simulators remains a major challenge, requiring new theoretical tools to model realistic, non-unitary evolution under continuous monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying quantum trajectory methods to model the conditional evolution of a Rydberg atom array under spatially-localized, continuous measurement will reveal distinct dynamical phases and a measurement-induced critical point, which can be engineered and probed using dynamically field-programmable gates.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework combining quantum trajectory (Monte Carlo wavefunction) methods with tensor network algorithms to model open-system dynamics of 1D and 2D Rydberg atom systems. The model will incorporate realistic dissipation and site-resolved measurement back-action, as informed by experimental parameters from neutral-atom processors. We will specifically adapt the 'dynamically field-programmable' circuit paradigm to include probabilistic measurement operations, enabling the study of entanglement growth and purification dynamics across many trajectories. The numerical analysis will focus on extracting order parameters and critical exponents for the MIPT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed, realistic theoretical blueprint for observing measurement-induced physics in near-term neutral-atom quantum processors. It will establish a concrete link between quantum trajectory theory and experimental protocols for Hamiltonian engineering of interacting qudit systems under monitoring. The results will guide future experiments aiming to use measurement back-action as a resource for quantum state preparation and error suppression.
proposals.novelty: While quantum trajectory methods are standard in quantum optics, their application to analyze MIPTs in dynamically programmable, interacting Rydberg arrays with long-range interactions and multi-level qudit structure is novel. This approach moves beyond idealized stochastic circuit models to address the specific open-system dynamics relevant to Lukin-group experimental platforms.

proposals.title: Deterministic Single-Photon Absorption via Rydberg Blockade in a Programmable Atomic Array
proposals.motivation: Efficient, deterministic single-photon detection and absorption is a critical challenge in quantum networking and optical quantum computing. Current single-photon detectors based on superconducting nanowires or avalanche photodiodes suffer from inefficiency, dark counts, and lack of photon-number resolution. A deterministic absorber would enable novel quantum memory interfaces and herald-free linear optical quantum computing protocols. Neutral atom arrays with Rydberg interactions offer a promising platform to address this challenge through collective quantum effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strong, long-range Rydberg blockade within a tightly packed ensemble of neutral atoms can be engineered to create a deterministic, single-photon absorbing medium, where the absorption of a first photon fundamentally prevents the absorption of a second.
proposals.method: We will use a dynamically field-programmable array of Rb-87 atoms, optically trapped and cooled. A specific target atom will be prepared in a Rydberg state, creating a blockade volume that shifts the collective excitation levels of a nearby, tightly packed ensemble of atoms (the 'absorber' ensemble). We will design and compile quantum circuits to implement a sequence of gates: first, a global microwave or optical pulse to prepare the absorber ensemble in a superradiant, collectively enhanced coupling state to a target intermediate level; second, a probe photon pulse resonant with the transition from this intermediate level to the Rydberg level. The blockade ensures only one collective excitation (i.e., one photon absorption event) can occur, which will be read out via state-selective fluorescence imaging of the entire array.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel, deterministic single-photon absorption mechanism with intrinsic photon-number resolution, directly leveraging Rydberg blockade for a quantum optics application. It will provide a foundational component for all-optical switching and quantum non-demolition photon detection, advancing the toolkit for integrated quantum systems based on neutral atoms.
proposals.novelty: While Rydberg blockade is well-established for creating photonic nonlinearities and single-photon sources, its application to create a deterministic single-photon *absorber* or detector in a programmable atomic array is a novel inversion of the typical paradigm, moving from photon generation to photon capture with built-in number resolution.

proposals.title: Generalized Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks for Classifying Diverse Quantum Phases of Matter
proposals.motivation: Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs) have shown promise for recognizing symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases, but their application to broader classes of quantum phases remains largely unexplored. Classifying phases like gapless critical points, symmetry-breaking orders, or topological orders beyond SPTs is a fundamental challenge in quantum many-body physics. A generalizable QCNN framework would provide a powerful, unified tool for quantum phase characterization, directly applicable to near-term quantum simulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the QCNN architecture can be generalized, through tailored circuit ansatzes and training on entanglement features, to successfully classify a wide spectrum of quantum phases, including those with long-range order, topological order, and criticality.
proposals.method: We will develop a programmable QCNN on a neutral-atom array processor, using Rydberg qubits and dynamically configurable gates. The circuit ansatz will be generalized beyond the fixed, hierarchical structure of the original QCNN by incorporating variational layers inspired by the Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA) to capture diverse entanglement scaling. We will train the model using Hamiltonian engineering techniques to prepare representative states of target phases (e.g., Z2 spin liquid, charge density wave, critical Ising) and employ classical co-processing to optimize the variational parameters for phase discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish QCNNs as a versatile tool for quantum matter analysis, moving beyond the proof-of-concept for SPT phases. It will deliver concrete, optimized circuit templates for classifying several distinct phase families on a programmable quantum simulator. The results will bridge quantum machine learning and condensed matter physics, providing a new methodological pathway for exploring phase diagrams of complex Hamiltonians.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving QCNNs from a specialized decoder for a single phase family to a flexible, variational architecture for multiple phase classifications, and in its direct implementation on a dynamically programmable neutral-atom platform, which allows for the necessary Hamiltonian engineering and circuit compilation.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryogenic Superconducting Qubit Control to NV-Nuclear Register Initialization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the primary benefit of millikelvin operation for NV-nuclear registers is merely extended coherence times. Drawing an analogy from superconducting quantum computing, where cryogenic operation is essential for active control and initialization, we adapt that field's solution of integrating specialized control electronics (e.g., cryogenic amplifiers, filters, and generators) into the refrigeration stages. The adapted research investigates the performance trade-offs of NV-nuclear registers at millikelvin temperatures by designing and testing integrated, cryogenically-compatible microwave/RF hardware for direct electron spin control. This approach aims to determine if such active, in-situ control at ultra-low temperatures can significantly improve nuclear spin polarization efficiency and two-qubit gate fidelity, rather than just passively benefiting from a colder bath.

ideas.title: Closed-loop resonant spin lock for high-fidelity non-optical NV measurement
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the critical SNR flaw by combining resonant microwave or magnetic field driving with a quantum-limited parametric amplifier for readout. An active feedback loop uses the amplified signal to apply corrective drives that dynamically lock the NV spin into a polarized state, simultaneously performing initialization and measurement. This closed-loop approach suppresses environmental noise and amplifies the inherently weak electrical signal, enabling single-shot readout fidelities that can surpass the standard optical threshold for fault-tolerant quantum error correction.

ideas.title: Feedback-Characterized and Compensated Engineered Dissipation
ideas.description: This approach first employs high-resolution quantum gas microscopy to perform non-destructive, site-resolved measurements of particle number, creating a precise spatial map of any unintended, 'background' loss in the system. This map then informs a real-time feedback loop using programmable optical potentials to either compensate for inhomogeneities or to redefine the regions where engineered dissipation is applied, ensuring a uniform and well-characterized open quantum environment. The system thus enables the study of open many-body dynamics with a dissipation profile that is both precisely known and tunable, separating intrinsic many-body effects from technical noise. This turns a major experimental weakness—uncontrolled loss—into a characterized and corrected parameter, drastically improving reproducibility and interpretability.

ideas.title: Probing topological excitations and dynamics via a gauged dual of the quantum Ising model
ideas.description: This research implements the dual (e.g., Wegner-Wilson loop) formulation of a 2D quantum Ising model in a transverse field, but with the explicit goal of isolating and measuring the properties of its topological excitations (visons or magnetic monopoles). The system is designed to numerically compute dynamical correlation functions in the dual gauge theory that correspond to experimentally accessible spectral responses in the original spin model, which are difficult to access directly. By comparing results across the duality, the work tests specific predictions about the confinement-deconfinement transition of these excitations and their signature in quantum simulators, turning a formal transformation into a tool for extracting new physical observables.

ideas.title: Hamiltonian Tomography via Dynamical Echoes and Spectral Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the core principle of tomography—solving an inverse problem by probing a system and inverting its response—from fields like medical imaging and seismology to quantum many-body physics. The analogous solution involves using controlled input signals to generate measurable output patterns, which are then computationally inverted to map internal structure. Here, the 'internal structure' is the Hamiltonian (its coupling parameters and disorder). The adapted method proposes initiating a quantum many-body system in a tailored, known state (or applying a known perturbation) and then meticulously observing the temporal evolution of a set of observables. This rich dynamical data serves as the 'echo' or 'transmission pattern.' Advanced inversion techniques, inspired by tomographic reconstruction algorithms but reformulated for quantum operator spaces and Lie algebras, are then developed to infer the Hamiltonian parameters and disorder distribution that best reproduce the observed dynamics, effectively performing 'Hamiltonian tomography.'

ideas.title: Dynamical Criticality and Phase Classification in Driven-Dissipative Systems with Continuous Symmetries
ideas.description: This research program focuses on non-equilibrium quantum systems, such as driven open Bose gases or arrays of non-linear resonators, where a continuous U(1) or SU(2) symmetry is broken spontaneously under driven-dissipative conditions. The core objective is to map and classify the distinct non-equilibrium steady states and their dynamical critical phenomena, which are not described by equilibrium Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson theory. This will involve developing new numerical tensor network methods for open quantum systems and identifying measurable signatures, like unique spectral features or correlation scaling laws, that distinguish these dynamical phases from their equilibrium counterparts.

ideas.title: Apply Richardson extrapolation from computational fluid dynamics to quantum error mitigation
ideas.description: This idea adapts Richardson extrapolation, a classical error mitigation technique from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), to the domain of digital quantum processors. In CFD, Richardson extrapolation estimates the exact solution of a differential equation by solving it on multiple grids of different fineness (mesh sizes) and mathematically extrapolating the results to the limit of infinitely fine grid spacing (zero discretization error). Analogously, for a noisy quantum computer, one can execute the same quantum circuit multiple times under different, known levels of amplified noise (e.g., by stretching gate times or inserting identity operations). By measuring the observable of interest at each noise level and fitting the results to a noise model, one can extrapolate the expectation value back to the hypothetical zero-noise limit. This provides an error-mitigated estimate of the true quantum computational result, effectively transferring a proven numerical analysis technique for suppressing discretization artifacts to the problem of suppressing physical noise artifacts in quantum systems.

ideas.title: Implement the dual of the quantum Ising model using a Fourier-space transformation analogous to optics
ideas.description: This research seed expands the study of the dual of the Ising model by drawing an analogy from optics and signal processing. In those fields, the powerful duality between real space and Fourier/reciprocal space is routinely used to transform complex problems into simpler, complementary representations (e.g., where convolutions become multiplications). The analogous solution is to apply a Fourier-transform-like operation. We adapt this by implementing a discrete Fourier or similar unitary transformation on the quantum Ising Hamiltonian. This maps the original spin operators to dual operators in a conjugate space, effectively creating a 'momentum-space' dual quantum model. Exploring this complementary physics could simplify the analysis of critical points, reveal hidden symmetries, and provide new numerical techniques for studying quantum phase transitions by working in the domain where the Hamiltonian may become diagonal or take a more tractable form.

ideas.title: Error-Adaptive Dynamical Decoupling via Embedded Sensor Qubits
ideas.description: This approach integrates a robust, closed-loop control system to overcome the fatal sensitivity of advanced DD sequences to systematic pulse errors. A dedicated sensor qubit, embedded within the same nuclear spin environment, continuously monitors control field fidelity and characterizes amplitude, phase, and timing imperfections. This diagnostic information feeds into an adaptive controller that dynamically adjusts the parameters of subsequent pulses in real-time, compensating for errors before they accumulate. The result is a self-correcting DD sequence that maintains its protective efficacy over extended durations, making coherence extension far more robust to experimental imperfections.

ideas.title: Modular 3D-Integrated Platform with Crosstalk-Managed NV Register Control
ideas.description: This redesigned platform implements a modular, 3D-stacked architecture for chip-scale NV center quantum registers. It separates the microwave control, optical excitation, and spin readout circuits onto distinct, vertically integrated layers interconnected by shielded through-silicon vias. Dedicated insulating interposer layers with embedded thermal microchannels and electromagnetic shielding are placed between active layers to suppress thermal and RF/optical crosstalk. This approach maintains the benefits of integration while preserving the low-noise environment essential for long NV center coherence and high-fidelity quantum control.

ideas.title: Calibrated Disorder Engineering via Closed-Loop Spatial Characterization
ideas.description: This approach transforms static disorder from an unknown variable into a calibrated, programmable resource. A suite of high-throughput diagnostic tools first constructs a detailed, quantitative spatial map of the intrinsic disorder present in an array. This map is then fed into a control system that can either apply corrective biases to mitigate disorder effects for uniform operations or, conversely, deliberately modulate qubits to enact a desired, reproducible disordered Hamiltonian. The result is a platform where disorder-aware protocols can be reliably executed, enabling the study of disorder-driven phases or error-resilient algorithms with known, characterized disorder as an input parameter.

ideas.title: Inference-Based Computational De-biasing via Learned Probabilistic Error Models
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond direct analogies to quantum techniques by developing a general framework for mitigating errors in classical analog or noisy digital processors. It operates by passively profiling the hardware under a range of ambient conditions to learn a joint probability model linking inputs, true outputs, and observed noisy outputs. This learned model then enables a Bayesian inference step to de-bias final computational results, effectively separating signal from systematic hardware-induced distortion. The method is applicable to systems where noise is not directly controllable but is statistically observable, making it more widely relevant than zero-noise extrapolation.

ideas.title: A Dimension-Agnostic, Hierarchically Interconnected Programmable Fabric with Dynamic Bypass Networks
ideas.description: This idea rethinks spatial computing arrays by decoupling the logical computation model from the strict physical lattice geometry. The core is a programmable fabric of processing elements that can be logically addressed in N dimensions, but which is physically enhanced with one or more reconfigurable long-range interconnect networks operating at different distance scales. A unified compiler and runtime system maps computational graphs onto this fabric, strategically placing data and scheduling operations to leverage both local neighbor communication for regularity and the dynamic bypass networks to break communication bottlenecks. The result is a system that preserves the conceptual simplicity and locality benefits of a spatial lattice while achieving the performance and scalability needed for real-world, irregular high-dimensional problems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Pathway Control for Adiabatic Phase Transitions
ideas.description: This research idea investigates dynamics from slower transitions across phase boundaries by drawing an analogy from protein folding. In biochemistry, molecular chaperones solve the analogous problem of achieving correct, defect-free states by providing a controlled environment that prevents kinetic traps during slow folding. Adapting this solution, the project proposes to design and apply a time-dependent, spatially-modulated external field—a 'dynamical chaperone'—during a slow quench. This field would gently reshape the system's energy landscape to suppress defect nucleation (e.g., topological defects or domain walls) and guide the system more faithfully towards the target adiabatic ground state, contrasting the outcomes with rapid quenches where such defects proliferate.

ideas.title: Adapting Isotope Purification from Semiconductor Silicon for 12C Diamond Growth
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that naturally abundant diamond must be used, proposing instead to engineer diamond from isotopically purified carbon. The analogy comes from Semiconductor Materials Engineering, where isotopically purified 28Si is produced via techniques like gas centrifugation of silane to eliminate 29Si nuclear spins for superior quantum coherence. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to apply advanced carbon isotope separation (e.g., on methane or CO) to create a 12C-enriched feedstock, which is then used in CVD reactors to synthesize isotopically pure 12C diamond. This material-by-design approach provides a scalable route to fabricate diamond quantum registers where the detrimental magnetic noise from 13C nuclear spins is absent.

ideas.title: Degeneracy-Aware, Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning via Optimal Experimental Design
ideas.description: This approach reframes Hamiltonian learning as an adaptive, closed-loop experiment where measurement strategy is co-designed with inference to overcome inherent degeneracies. The system begins with an initial set of dynamical observations and a Bayesian posterior over Hamiltonian parameters. An algorithmic controller then calculates the next most informative observable to measure—maximizing expected information gain—and directs the experimental or simulation apparatus to collect that data. This process iteratively breaks degeneracies by strategically probing the system's response, ensuring convergence to a unique Hamiltonian. The final output is not just a point estimate but a validated, high-confidence posterior distribution for all parameters and disorder profiles.

ideas.title: Cellular Automaton-Inspired Hierarchical Lattice for Programmable Spatial Arrays
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea of implementing a 2D or 3D lattice geometry for a programmable array by drawing an analogy from cellular automata in complex systems theory. The analogous solution uses a regular grid of simple, identical cells whose state evolves based on uniform rules applied to a local neighborhood, enabling scalable, decentralized computation of complex global patterns. The adapted idea applies this principle to hardware or simulation, constructing the lattice as a grid of basic programmable units. Each unit's function is defined by a rule set that processes inputs from its immediate von Neumann or Moore neighbors (in 2D) or their 3D equivalents. This architecture inherently supports spatial algorithms, pattern generation, and morphological operations, making the array's scaling dual—both in physical size and computational complexity—driven by local interactions rather than global control, thus extending programmability fundamentally into two or three spatial dimensions.

ideas.title: Adapting Liquid Crystal Topological Defect Engineering to Continuous Symmetry Breaking Models
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to study phases with continuous symmetry breaking by drawing an analogy from soft matter physics, specifically the study of liquid crystals. In nematic liquid crystals, continuous rotational symmetry is broken, leading to a director field with orientational order and characteristic topological defects (disclinations). The analogous solution involves using homotopy theory to classify defects, continuum elastic theory to model their behavior, and combined experimental/computational approaches to study their role in phase transitions and dynamics. Adapted to the original context, this suggests a research program to systematically apply the liquid crystal defect-engineering toolkit to canonical models of continuous symmetry breaking, such as the O(N) models. The goal is to develop a unified framework for predicting, characterizing, and potentially manipulating topological defects (like vortices, skyrmions, or monopoles) that arise when a continuous symmetry is spontaneously broken, moving beyond the discrete domain walls of Ising-type systems. This approach would combine analytical classification via homotopy groups, numerical simulation of defect dynamics in lattice models, and the study of how defects influence critical phenomena and non-equilibrium behavior.
